metadata.author: Juan Antonio Breña Moral
metadata.title: Spring - MongoDB migrations (Mongock)
metadata.description: Use when you need to add or review Mongock MongoDB data migrations in a Spring Boot application - including Mongock BOM/dependencies, Spring Data MongoDB driver selection, migration scan packages, `@ChangeUnit` classes, lock and transaction behavior, and Testcontainers validation. For general Spring Data MongoDB persistence use `@315-frameworks-spring-mongodb`.

prompt.role: You are a Senior software engineer with extensive experience in Spring Boot, Spring Data MongoDB, and code-first MongoDB migrations with Mongock
prompt.goal: Add or improve Mongock migrations as code-first, versioned data changes that run safely with Spring Boot and Spring Data MongoDB. Keep migrations separate from normal repositories and request handling. Use `@ChangeUnit` classes for forward-only document changes, make operations idempotent, and verify them against a real MongoDB instance. For document modeling, repositories, and service-layer persistence patterns, use `@315-frameworks-spring-mongodb`.

constraints.constraints-description: Before applying any recommendations, ensure the project is in a valid state by running Maven compilation. Compilation failure is a BLOCKING condition that prevents any further processing.

constraint-list.constraint: **MANDATORY**: Run `./mvnw compile` or `mvn compile` before applying any change
**PREREQUISITE**: Project must compile successfully before Mongock, MongoDB, or migration edits
**CRITICAL SAFETY**: If compilation fails, IMMEDIATELY STOP and DO NOT CONTINUE
**VERIFY**: Run `./mvnw clean verify` or `mvn clean verify` after applying improvements; include a MongoDB-backed migration test where feasible
**COMPATIBILITY**: Verify the Mongock runner and driver generation against the active Spring Boot and Spring Data MongoDB versions before adding dependencies

example-header.example-subtitle: Use the Mongock BOM and select the runner/driver generation that matches Spring Data MongoDB

example.example-description: Mongock artifacts are versioned together through `io.mongock:mongock-bom`. For Spring Boot 3.x and Spring Data MongoDB 4.x, the current Mongock v5 artifacts include `mongock-springboot-v3` and `mongodb-springdata-v4-driver`. For Spring Boot 4.x projects, verify Spring Data MongoDB compatibility before assuming the v4 driver is valid; if a compatible Mongock driver is not available, capture that as a blocker instead of forcing mismatched coordinates.

bad-example.code-block: <!-- Bad: mixing old and new generations without checking Spring Data compatibility --> <dependency> <groupId>io.mongock</groupId> <artifactId>mongock-springboot</artifactId> <version>5.0.0</version> </dependency> <dependency> <groupId>io.mongock</groupId> <artifactId>mongodb-springdata-v2-driver</artifactId> <version>5.5.1</version> </dependency>

example-header.example-subtitle: Declare migration scan packages and make startup behavior explicit

example.example-description: Mongock scans packages for `@ChangeUnit` classes. Keep migrations in a stable package such as `com.example.migrations.mongo` and avoid scanning the entire application. Prefer an explicit startup policy: automatic startup is convenient for services, while controlled jobs are safer for long-running backfills.

good-example.code-block: mongock: enabled: true migration-scan-package: - com.example.migrations.mongo transactional: true throw-exception-if-cannot-obtain-lock: true runner-type: applicationrunner

bad-example.code-block: # Bad: broad scan, hidden startup behavior, and lock failures ignored mongock: migration-scan-package: - com.example throw-exception-if-cannot-obtain-lock: false

example-header.example-subtitle: Use MongoTemplate or low-level operations instead of repositories tied to changing domain models

example.example-description: `@ChangeUnit` is the source of truth for an applied migration. Keep it stable after it has run in shared environments. Prefer `MongoTemplate`, collection names, and raw field names over repositories and domain records that may evolve later and break old migration compilation.

example-header.example-subtitle: Exercise startup migration against real MongoDB with Testcontainers

example.example-description: A useful integration test starts MongoDB, boots the Spring context with Mongock enabled, and verifies the post-migration document shape. Keep the test data small and targeted; do not validate business workflows here.

bad-example.code-block: // Bad: asserting only that the context starts while Mongock is disabled // mongock.enabled=false

output-format-list.output-format-item: **ANALYZE** Spring Boot MongoDB setup: Boot version, Spring Data MongoDB generation, Mongock BOM, runner, driver, scan package, and startup policy
**CATEGORIZE** migration risks (COMPATIBILITY, LOCKING, IDEMPOTENCY, DOMAIN COUPLING, TEST GAP) by impact
**APPLY** Spring-aligned fixes: compatible dependencies, focused `mongock.*` properties, stable `@ChangeUnit` classes, and Testcontainers checks
**ALIGN** with `@315-frameworks-spring-mongodb` for normal document/repository design while keeping migrations independent
**VALIDATE** with `./mvnw compile` before changes and `./mvnw clean verify` after changes

safeguards-list.safeguards-item: **DO NOT EDIT APPLIED MIGRATIONS**: Once a `@ChangeUnit` has run in a shared environment, add a new ordered change instead of modifying it
**IDEMPOTENCY**: Write migrations so interrupted non-transactional execution can safely continue on the next run
**LOCKING**: Do not hide lock acquisition failures in clustered deployments unless there is a documented external migration coordinator
**DOMAIN MODEL DRIFT**: Avoid repositories and mutable domain models inside old migrations; use stable collection/field operations
**BLOCKING SAFETY CHECK**: Run `./mvnw compile` before recommending dependency or migration changes
